From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft&redirect=no "Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft"))

Former German national railway system

This article is about the national railways of Germany between the two world wars. For state railways of the post-war East Germany, see [Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany) "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)").

Deutsche Reichsbahn

The ***Deutsche Reichsbahn*** (German pronunciation: [[ˈdɔʏtʃə ˈʁaɪçsˌbaːn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")), also known as the **German National Railway**,[[1]](#cite_note-ACMi-1) the **German State Railway**, **German Reich Railway**,[[2]](#cite_note-TZ-2) and the **German Imperial Railway**,[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-IAn-4) was the [German](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") national [railway](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") system created after the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") from the regional railways of the individual states of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). The *Deutsche Reichsbahn* has been described as "the largest enterprise in the capitalist world in the years between 1920 and 1932";[[1]](#cite_note-ACMi-1) nevertheless, its importance "arises primarily from the fact that the Reichsbahn was at the center of events in a period of great turmoil in German history".[[1]](#cite_note-ACMi-1)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The company was founded on 1 April 1920 as the *Deutsche Reichseisenbahnen* ("German Imperial Railways")[[1]](#cite_note-ACMi-1) when the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), which still used the nation-state term of the previous monarchy, *[Deutsches Reich](/wiki/Deutsches_Reich "Deutsches Reich")* (German Reich, hence the usage of the *[Reich](/wiki/Reich "Reich")* in the name of the railway; the monarchical term was *Deutsches Kaiserreich*), took national control of the German railways, which had previously been run by the German states (*[Länderbahnen](/wiki/L%C3%A4nderbahnen "Länderbahnen")*). In 1924 it was reorganised under the aegis of the *Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft* ("German Imperial Railway Company", DRG), a nominally private railway company, which was 100% owned by the German state. In 1937 the railway was reorganised again as a state authority and given the name *Deutsche Reichsbahn* ("German Imperial Railway", DRB). After the *[Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")* in 1938 the DR also took over the *[Bundesbahn Österreich](/wiki/Bundesbahn_%C3%96sterreich "Bundesbahn Österreich")* ("Federal Railway of Austria", BBÖ).[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The [East](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") and [West](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") German states were founded in 1949. East Germany took over the control of the DR on its territory and continued to use the traditional name *[Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany) "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)")*, while the railway in West Germany became the *[Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn")* ("German Federal Railway", DB). The [Austrian](/wiki/Austria "Austria") *[Österreichische Bundesbahnen](/wiki/%C3%96BB "ÖBB")* ("Austrian Federal Railways", ÖBB) was founded in 1945, and was given its present name in 1947.

In January 1994, following [German reunification](/wiki/Reunification_of_Germany "Reunification of Germany"), the East German *[Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany) "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)")* merged with the West German *[Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn")* to form Germany's new national carrier, *[Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn") AG* ("German Rail", DBAG), technically no longer a government agency but still a 100% state-owned [joint stock company](/wiki/Joint_stock_company "Joint stock company").

## Deutsche Reichseisenbahnen (1920–1924)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Deutsche Reichseisenbahnen (1920–1924)")]

The first railways to be owned by the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), which was founded in 1871, were the [Imperial Railways in Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Imperial_Railways_in_Alsace-Lorraine "Imperial Railways in Alsace-Lorraine"), whose Imperial General Division of Railways in Alsace-Lorraine (*Kaiserliche General-Direktion der Eisenbahnen in Elsass-Lothringen*) had its headquarters in [Straßburg](/wiki/Stra%C3%9Fburg "Straßburg") (now Strasbourg). It was formed after France had ceded the territory of [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") in 1871 to the German Empire and the newly created [Third French Republic](/wiki/Third_French_Republic "Third French Republic") had formally purchased the [French Eastern Railway Company](/wiki/French_Eastern_Railway_Company "French Eastern Railway Company") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Compagnie des chemins de fer de l'Est* or [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Französische Ostbahn-Gesellschaft*) and then sold it again to the German Empire. After the end of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") this national "imperial railway" was taken back by France.

In the remaining German states, by contrast, the existing state railways continued to be subject to their respective sovereigns, despite the fact that [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") had tried in vain to purchase the main railway lines for the Empire. A similar attempt failed in 1875 as a result of opposition from the [middle powers](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") when [Albert von Maybach](/wiki/Albert_von_Maybach "Albert von Maybach") presented a draft Reich Railway Act to the [Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_(German_Empire) "Bundesrat (German Empire)").

In the wake of the stipulations of the [Weimar Constitution](/wiki/Weimar_Constitution "Weimar Constitution") of 11 August 1919, the state treaty on the foundation of the *Deutsche Reichseisenbahnen* ("German Reich Railways") came into force on 1 April 1920. This resulted in the merger of the existing state railways (*[Länderbahnen](/wiki/L%C3%A4nderbahnen "Länderbahnen")*) of [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"), [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony"), [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg"), [Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden"), [Mecklenburg](/wiki/Mecklenburg "Mecklenburg") and [Oldenburg](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Oldenburg "Grand Duchy of Oldenburg") under the newly formed [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich "German Reich"). The state railways that merged were the:

* [Baden state railways](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden_State_Railways "Grand Duchy of Baden State Railways")
* [Mecklenburg state railways](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Mecklenburg_Friedrich-Franz_Railway "Grand Duchy of Mecklenburg Friedrich-Franz Railway")
* [Oldenburg state railways](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Oldenburg_State_Railways "Grand Duchy of Oldenburg State Railways")
* [Bavarian state railways](/wiki/Royal_Bavarian_State_Railways "Royal Bavarian State Railways")
* [Saxon state railways](/wiki/Royal_Saxon_State_Railways "Royal Saxon State Railways")
* [Württemberg state railways](/wiki/Royal_W%C3%BCrttemberg_State_Railways "Royal Württemberg State Railways")
* [Prussian-Hessian state railways](/wiki/Prussian-Hessian_Railway_Company "Prussian-Hessian Railway Company")

Initially called the *Reichseisenbahnen* or *Deutsche Reichseisenbahnen*, the company was formally given the name "Deutsche Reichsbahn" by decree of the Reich Minister of Transport, [Wilhelm Groener](/wiki/Wilhelm_Groener "Wilhelm Groener"), on 27 June 1921. In 1922 the old railway divisions (*Eisenbahndirektionen*) were renamed as Reich railway divisions (*[Reichsbahndirektionen](/wiki/Reichsbahndirektion "Reichsbahndirektion")*).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft (1924–1937)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft (1924–1937)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-01342%2C_Deutsche_Reichsbahn%2C_Zugf%C3%BChrer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-01342%2C_Deutsche_Reichsbahn%2C_Zugf%C3%BChrer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-01342,_Deutsche_Reichsbahn,_Zugf%C3%BChrer.jpg)

A DRG [conductor](/wiki/Conductor_(transportation) "Conductor (transportation)") in 1928 complete with rank insignia

Among the provisions of the 1924 [Dawes Plan](/wiki/Dawes_Plan "Dawes Plan") was a plan to utilize the state railway completely for the payment of [war reparations](/wiki/World_War_I_reparations "World War I reparations"). Following the plan's publication, on 12 February 1924, the Reich government announced the creation of the Deutsche Reichsbahn as a state enterprise under the [Reich Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Reich_Ministry_of_Transport "Reich Ministry of Transport") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Reichsverkehrsministerium*).

As this was not enough to satisfy the reparations creditors, on 30 August 1924 a law was enacted providing for the establishment of a state-owned *Deutsche Reichsbahn-Gesellschaft* ("German Imperial Railway Company", DRG) as a public [holding company](/wiki/Holding_company "Holding company") to operate the national railways. The aim was to earn profits which, under the Dawes Plan, were to be used to contribute to Germany's war reparations.

At the same time as the Reichsbahn law was enacted, the company was handed a bill of eleven billion [Goldmarks](/wiki/German_gold_mark "German gold mark") to be paid to the Allied powers, while its original capital was valued at fifteen billion Goldmarks. These terms were later amended in the [Young Plan](/wiki/Young_Plan "Young Plan"). Nevertheless, the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and the regular payment of [war reparations](/wiki/War_reparations "War reparations") (about 660 million [Reichsmarks](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark") annually) put a considerable strain on the Reichsbahn. Not until the [Lausanne Conference of 1932](/wiki/Lausanne_Conference_of_1932 "Lausanne Conference of 1932") was the Reichsbahn released from its financial obligations. In total, about 3.87 billion Goldmarks was paid in reparations to the Allied powers.

During the DRG period the following milestones occurred:

* 1 October 1930: the DRG took over the Bremen Port Railway (*Hafenbahn Bremen*)
* 27 June 1933: the DRG's sister company the *[Reichsautobahn](/wiki/Reichsautobahn "Reichsautobahn")* was founded
* 1 March 1935: the railways of the Saar were incorporated

The beginning of the DRG was characterised by the acquisition of new rolling stock built to standard types, such as the standard steam locomotives (*[Einheitsdampflokomotiven](/wiki/Einheitsdampflokomotiven "Einheitsdampflokomotiven")*). The stock already in use had been inherited from the various state railways and comprised a great number of designs, many of them quite old. In fact, the DRG was unable to procure new stock in the numbers it wanted to both for financial reasons and due to delays in upgrading the lines to carry higher [axle loads](/wiki/Axle_load "Axle load"). The locomotive classes taken over from the old state railways, especially those from the Prussia, continued to dominate the scene until the end of the 1930s. They included, for example, the [Prussian P 8](/wiki/Prussian_P_8 "Prussian P 8") (BR 38.10-40), [Prussian P 10](/wiki/Prussian_P_10 "Prussian P 10") (BR 39), [Prussian G 12](/wiki/Prussian_G_12 "Prussian G 12") (BR 58.10) and the [Prussian T 20](/wiki/Prussian_T_20 "Prussian T 20") (BR 95). The [Bavarian S 3/6](/wiki/Bavarian_S_3/6 "Bavarian S 3/6") (BR 18.5) [express locomotive](/wiki/Express_locomotive "Express locomotive") even continued in production until 1930.

Not until the procurement programme for the wartime *[Kriegslokomotiven](/wiki/Kriegslokomotive "Kriegslokomotive")* were new goods locomotives built in large numbers, but of course now for a very different purpose.

Taking lead from the German Labor Front, the Deutsche Reichsbahn took part in the conflict of intermarriage in Germany. In August 1933 Robert Ley, leader of Reich Labor, demanded that those administrators working for the German Labor Front be married only to German individuals. The Deutsche Reichsbahn took the lead in discriminating against intermarried workers, firing German employees married to Jews and forbidding intermarried Germans from working there in the future, starting in November 1933.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1935 the railway network had a total of 68,728 kilometres (42,706 mi) of line, of which 30,330 km (18,850 mi) was [main line railway](/wiki/Main_line_railway "Main line railway"), 27,209 km (16,907 mi) were [branch lines](/wiki/Branch_line "Branch line") and 10,496 km (6,522 mi) were [light railways](/wiki/Light_railway "Light railway").[[11]](#cite_note-11) The vehicle fleet in 1926 comprised 27,600 locomotives, 63,900 passenger cars, 690,000 freight cars, 21,800 luggage cars, and 1,000 motor locomotives.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In the latter part of the 1930s, the development of high-speed trains like the ["Flying Hamburger"](/wiki/DRG_Class_877 "DRG Class 877") was accelerated. Before that streamlined steam engines had been built, but they were not as economical as the high-speed diesel and electric railcars. Although the Borsig streamlined steam engine, the [no. 05 002](/wiki/DRG_Class_05 "DRG Class 05") reached a speed of 200.4 km/h (124.5 mph) during a demonstration run, the Reichsbahn preferred fast railcars on its high speed network. The potential of these express trains was demonstrated by the *[Schienenzeppelin](/wiki/Schienenzeppelin "Schienenzeppelin")* in its record run on 21 June 1931 when it reached a top speed of 230.2 km/h (143.0 mph).

Before the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") the most important railway lines ran in an east–west direction. The high-speed lines at that time were on the [Prussian Eastern Railway](/wiki/Prussian_Eastern_Railway "Prussian Eastern Railway") which ran through the [Polish corridor](/wiki/Polish_corridor "Polish corridor") (albeit slower there due to the poor state of the tracks), the lines from [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") to [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), via [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover") to the [Ruhrgebiet](/wiki/Ruhrgebiet "Ruhrgebiet"), via [Frankfurt am Main](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") to southwest Germany, on which the diesel express trains ran, and the [Silesian Railway](/wiki/Lower_Silesian-Mark_Railway "Lower Silesian-Mark Railway") from Berlin to [Breslau](/wiki/Breslau "Breslau") (now Wrocław).

### Bavarian Group Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bavarian Group Administration")]

Within the state of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), the [Bavarian Group Administration](/wiki/Bavarian_Group_Administration "Bavarian Group Administration") (*Gruppenverwaltung Bayern*) had its head office (*Zentrales Maschinen- und Bauamt*) and was largely independent by § III 14 of the DRG's company regulations. It was responsible for the electrification of many lines, following the commencement of [electric power generation to the railways](/wiki/Railway_electrification_system "Railway electrification system") at the [Walchensee Power Plant](/wiki/Walchensee_Power_Plant "Walchensee Power Plant"), and for the independent trialling and procurement of locomotives and passenger coaches. The Group Administration introduced, for example, the [Class E 32](/wiki/DR_Class_E_32 "DR Class E 32") locomotive and [Class ET 85](/wiki/DR_Class_ET_85 "DR Class ET 85") railcar into service.

Bavaria also continued to use its own [signalling system](/wiki/Bavarian_railway_signals "Bavarian railway signals") for many years after the merger.

In 1933 the Group Administration was disbanded and administration of the railways in Bavaria was taken over by the Deutsche Reichsbahn.

### Electrification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Electrification")]

Between 1901 and 1904 first electric high-speed pilot vehicles were proven in Zossen/ Berlin [Marienfelde station](/wiki/Marienfelde_station "Marienfelde station"). The [electrification](/wiki/Electrification "Electrification") of many lines started with local grids, so in Bavaria (Augsburg), Saxony ([Central Germany (cultural area)](/wiki/Central_Germany_(cultural_area) "Central Germany (cultural area)"), Berlin (pilot lines) and in [Lower Silesia](/wiki/Lower_Silesia "Lower Silesia"). 1911 the first 15 kV AC (16.7 Hz) line was erected between Dessau and Bitterfeld including the power plant Muldenstein. 1914 started the electrified Silesian grid with the power plant Mittelsteine.

### Leadership of the Reichsbahn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Leadership of the Reichsbahn")]

At the head of the Reichsbahn was a director general (*Generaldirektor*). The office holders were:

* 1924–1926 [Rudolf Oeser](/wiki/Rudolf_Oeser_(politician) "Rudolf Oeser (politician)")
* 1926–1945 [Julius Dorpmüller](/wiki/Julius_Dorpm%C3%BCller "Julius Dorpmüller")

From 1925, the director general had a permanent deputy. These were:

* 1925–1926 Julius Dorpmüller
* 1926–1933 Wilhelm Weirauch
* 1933–1942 [Wilhelm Kleinmann](/wiki/Wilhelm_Kleinmann "Wilhelm Kleinmann")
* 1942–1945 [Albert Ganzenmüller](/wiki/Albert_Ganzenm%C3%BCller "Albert Ganzenmüller")

As a result of the Reichsbahn Act of 11 July 1939, the Reich Transport Minister became the director general of the Reichsbahn by his office. Dorpmüller, who since 1937 was also in charge of the [Reich Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Reich_Ministry_of_Transport "Reich Ministry of Transport"), continued in office as the director general after 1939 under this new legal framework.

## Deutsche Reichsbahn (1937–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Deutsche Reichsbahn (1937–1945)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Milit%C3%A4rfahrkarte1938.jpg/250px-Milit%C3%A4rfahrkarte1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milit%C3%A4rfahrkarte1938.jpg)

1938 military ticket from [Rendsburg](/wiki/Rendsburg "Rendsburg") to [Königsberg (Pr.)](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg_(Preu%C3%9Fen) "Königsberg (Preußen)")

With the Act for the New Regulation of the Conditions of the Reichsbank and the Deutsche Reichsbahn (*Gesetz zur Neuregelung der Verhältnisse der Reichsbank und der Deutschen Reichsbahn*) of 10 February 1937 the *Deutsche Reichsbahn Gesellschaft* was placed under Reich sovereignty and was given the name *Deutsche Reichsbahn*.

### World War II and military use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: World War II and military use")]

The *Reichsbahn* had an important logistic role in supporting the rapid movement of the troops of the [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht"), for example:

* March 1938: the [annexation of Austria (Anschluss)](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss") and
* October 1938: the annexation of the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") after the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement")
* March 1939: the [German occupation of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Czechoslovakia "German occupation of Czechoslovakia")
* September/October 1939: the [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland")
* April 1940: [Operation Weserübung](/wiki/Operation_Weser%C3%BCbung "Operation Weserübung") (the invasions of Denmark and Norway)
* May/June 1940: the [Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France")
* 1941: [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") and the [Balkan Campaign](/wiki/Balkan_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Balkan Campaign (World War II)").

In all the occupied lands the Reichsbahn endeavoured to incorporate the captured railways (rolling stock and infrastructure) into their system. Even towards the end of the war the Reichsbahn continued to move military formations. For example, in the last great offensive, the [Battle of the Bulge](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Bulge "Battle of the Bulge") (from 16 December 1944), tank formations were transported from [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") to the [Ardennes](/wiki/Ardennes "Ardennes").

The railways managed by the "Eastern Railway Division" (*Generaldirektion der Ostbahn*) were initially run from that part of the [Polish State Railways](/wiki/Polish_State_Railways "Polish State Railways") within the so-called [General Government](/wiki/General_Government "General Government")-assigned part of the [*Polnischen Staatsbahnen* (PKP)](/wiki/Polskie_Koleje_Pa%C5%84stwowe "Polskie Koleje Państwowe"), but from November 1939 by the Ostbahn (Generalgouvernement).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_%28German_Reich_Railway%29_Lantern_c1942.jpg/250px-Deutsche_Reichsbahn_%28German_Reich_Railway%29_Lantern_c1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(German_Reich_Railway)_Lantern_c1942.jpg)

WWII *Reichsbahn* military marked railwayman's carbide burner lantern (c. 1942)

In the campaigns against [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), France, [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") etc. the newly acquired [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") networks could be used without difficulty. By contrast, after the [start of the invasion of Russia](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") on 22 June 1941, the problem arose of transferring troops and materiel to Soviet [broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge") lines or converting them to German standard gauge. Confounding German plans, the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") and Soviet railways managed to withdraw or destroy the majority of its [rolling stock](/wiki/Rolling_stock "Rolling stock") during its [retreat](/wiki/Retreat_(military) "Retreat (military)"). As a result, German standard gauge rolling stock had to be used for an additional logistic role within Russia; this required the laying of standard gauge [track](/wiki/Track_(rail_transport) "Track (rail transport)"). The price was high: Reichsbahn railway staff and the [railway troops](/wiki/Railway_troops "Railway troops") of the [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") had to convert a total of 16,148 kilometres (10,034 mi) of Soviet trackage to German standard gauge track between 22 June and 8 October 1941.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

During the war, locomotives in the war zones were sometimes given camouflage livery. In addition, locomotives were painted with the *[Hoheitsadler](/wiki/Hoheitsadler "Hoheitsadler")* symbol (the eagle, Germany's traditional symbol of national [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty")) holding a [swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika"). On goods wagons the name "Deutsche Reichsbahn" was replaced by the letters "DR". Postal coaches continued to bear the name "Deutsche Reichspost".

The logistics of the *Reichsbahn* were crucial to the conduct of Germany's military offensives. The preparations for the invasion of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") saw the greatest troop deployment by rail in history.

### Holocaust

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Holocaust")]

Main article: [Holocaust trains](/wiki/Holocaust_trains "Holocaust trains")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/ISR-2015-Jerusalem-Yad_Vashem-Wagon_monument_01.jpg/330px-ISR-2015-Jerusalem-Yad_Vashem-Wagon_monument_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISR-2015-Jerusalem-Yad_Vashem-Wagon_monument_01.jpg)

The wagon monument, [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem"), Jerusalem

The [logistics](/wiki/Logistics "Logistics") of the Reichsbahn were also an important factor during the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"). [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") and other groups were transported like cattle to the [concentration](/wiki/Concentration_camp "Concentration camp") and [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") by the Deutsche Reichsbahn in [trains](/wiki/Train "Train") of [covered goods wagons](/wiki/Covered_goods_wagon "Covered goods wagon"), now known as [Holocaust trains](/wiki/Holocaust_trains "Holocaust trains"). These movements using [cattle wagons](/wiki/Cattle_wagon "Cattle wagon") from the goods station of the great [Frankfurt Market Hall](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fmarkthalle_(Frankfurt) "Großmarkthalle (Frankfurt)"), for example, thus played a significant role in the [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") within the extermination machinery of the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"). In 1997, the market erected a [memorial plaque](/wiki/Memorial_plaque "Memorial plaque") in recognition of this dark period of history.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The following is an excerpt from the testimony of Holocaust scholar [Raul Hilberg](/wiki/Raul_Hilberg "Raul Hilberg"):

> The *Reichsbahn* was ready to ship in principle any cargo in return for payment. And therefore, the basic key – price controlled key – was that Jews were going to be shipped to [Treblinka](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp"), were going to be shipped to [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz"), [Sobibor](/wiki/Sobibor_extermination_camp "Sobibor extermination camp") ... so long as the railroads were paid by the track kilometer, so many pfennigs per mile. The rate was the same throughout the war, with children under ten going at half-fare and children under four going free. Payment had to be made for only one way. The guards of course had to have return fare paid for them because they were going back to their place of origin ...[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Conditions in the wagons were inhumane because no water or food was provided, and sanitary arrangements were minimal, usually a bucket in a corner of the wagon. Although each wagon was intended to hold about 50 people, they were frequently overcrowded and holding 100 to 150 people. No heating was provided, so people could freeze in winter and overheat in summer. Deaths in the wagons were frequent among the young, old, sick, and disabled, especially as travel was slow and often lasted many days since the trains had low priority on the tracks. Their small amount of [luggage](/wiki/Luggage "Luggage") was stored separately, sometimes at the station and never left with the train, but examined for valuables which were stolen or resold for profit.

Beginning in November 2007, a museum train, the "Train of Commemoration" (*Zug der Erinnerung*), began a 3,000 km (1,900 mi) tour of Germany as a rolling memorial to the thousands of youth and children who were deported from all over Europe, many via the *Reichsbahn*, to the camps. A certain amount of controversy has surrounded the train's tour through Germany, in part because of the apparent lack of cooperation on the part of [*Deutsche Bahn* AG](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn") (DB AG) concerning such matters as compensation for the use of the DB AG's right of way (during the tour) and the stationing of the train, during its visit to [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), at the *[Ostbahnhof](/wiki/Berlin_Ostbahnhof "Berlin Ostbahnhof")* station instead of the more centrally located *[Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/Berlin_Hauptbahnhof "Berlin Hauptbahnhof")* main railway station. The tour was scheduled to end on 8 May 2008 (the 63rd anniversary of the end of the European portion of World War II) when the train arrived at Auschwitz. However, it continued to make appearances through 2009, and as of January 2010 the website requests visitors to look for further travel plans at the end of February.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Rebuilding after 1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rebuilding after 1945")]

German railways were heavily bombed by Allied [RAF](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force "Royal Air Force") and [USAAF](/wiki/USAAF "USAAF") bombers. Marshalling yards, bridges, repair shops, and service facilities were all destroyed. [Fighter-bombers](/wiki/Fighter-bomber "Fighter-bomber") targeted locomotives and bombed them. As a result, trains were at a standstill in the spring of 1945. The cities of Hamburg, Munich, Nuremberg, Frankfurt, Dusseldorf, Berlin, Leipzig, Dresden and others were affected. Stations were completely destroyed and wagons and carriage set on fire and destroyed. Bomb craters and blast seriously damaged the permanent way or rail track. The Allied forces of Occupation were put in charge and instantly had myriad problems regarding food, lack of housing, fuel, displaced persons and people on the move.

The Engineering Corps of British and American forces oversaw the partial rebuilding of the lines and cars with local labour from prisoners of war, [rubble women](/wiki/Tr%C3%BCmmerfrau "Trümmerfrau"), and de-mobilized soldiers. Temporary wooden bridges were put up over destroyed spans. Multiple tracks were disassembled into one smaller working line, equipment assessed and rebuilt. In three months, the railway was working again in a rudimentary form. The Armies of Occupation needed the railways to move coal and the soon to be gathered agricultural harvest. Deutschebahn had a critical shortage of wagons, carriages and locomotives, so much so that the US gave war surplus engines to ensure the movement of freight.

### Breakup of the Reichsbahn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Breakup of the Reichsbahn")]

With the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") in 1945 those parts of the Deutsche Reichsbahn that were outside the new German borders laid down in the [Potsdam Agreement](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreement "Potsdam Agreement") were transferred to the ownership and administration of the states in whose territory they were situated. For example, on 27 April 1945, the Austrian railways became independent again as the Austrian State Railway (*Österreichische Staatseisenbahn* or ÖStB), later renamed as the Austrian Federal Railways (*Österreichische Bundesbahnen* or [ÖBB](/wiki/%C3%96BB "ÖBB")) on 5 August 1947.

### Railways in the occupation zones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Railways in the occupation zones")]

Operational control of the rest of the DR was devolved to the respective [zones of occupation](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany") so that the *Reichsbahn* legally existed in four parts until 1949.

#### US Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: US Zone")]

In the [American Zone](/wiki/American_Zone "American Zone") the Reichsbahn divisions of Augsburg, Frankfurt am Main, Kassel, Munich, Regensburg and Stuttgart (for the railways in [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden")) were subordinated to the Senior Control Office US Zone (*Oberbetriebsleitung United States Zone*) in Frankfurt.

#### British Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: British Zone")]

The Reichsbahn divisions of Essen, Hamburg, Hanover, Cologne, Münster (Westfalen) and Wuppertal were grouped into the *Reichsbahn-Generaldirektion in the [British Zone](/wiki/British_occupation_zone_in_Germany "British occupation zone in Germany")* under Director General Max Leibbrand in Bielefeld.

#### French Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: French Zone")]

In the French Occupation Zone, the railways were grouped into the Operating Association of the Southwest German Railways (*Betriebsvereinigung der Südwestdeutschen Eisenbahnen*) with its headquarters in Speyer. The Operating Association included the railway divisions of Karlsruhe (in the US Zone), Mainz and Saarbrücken. After the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") was transferred from the French Zone and was given its own state railway – the Railways of the Saarland (*Eisenbahnen des Saarlandes*) – the rest of the network of the Saarbrücken division went into the new [Trier](/wiki/Trier "Trier") division. After the Deutsche Bundesbahn was formed this Operating Association was merged with it.

#### Soviet Zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Soviet Zone")]

The [Soviet zone of occupation](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_zone "Soviet occupation zone") became a self-declared socialist state, the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") (commonly known as East Germany), on 7 October 1949. One month prior, on 7 September 1949, the railway systems in the three western zones (the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany")), were reunified and renamed the *[Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn")* (DB – German Federal Railways).

On the formation of East Germany on 7 October 1949, the railway system in the Soviet Zone retained the name *[Deutsche Reichsbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany) "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)")* (DR), despite the connotations of the word "Reich". This was due to the designation of the *Reichsbahn* in postwar treaties and military protocols as the railway operator in [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin"), a role it retained until the creation of the unified DBAG at the beginning of 1994.

#### Bizone and creation of the DB

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Bizone and creation of the DB")]

To conform to the formation of the [Bizone](/wiki/Bizone "Bizone") in 1946 the Head Office of the Railways of the American and British Occupation Regions (*Hauptverwaltung der Eisenbahnen des amerikanischen und britischen Besatzungsgebiets*) was created. In 1947 it moved its headquarters to [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main") and called itself the Deutsche Reichsbahn in the United Economic Region (*Deutsche Reichsbahn im Vereinigten Wirtschaftsgebiet*). Following the foundation of the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany"), it was renamed [Deutsche Bundesbahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbahn "Deutsche Bundesbahn").

### East German Deutsche Reichsbahn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Reichsbahn&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: East German Deutsche Reichsbahn")]

Main article: [Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany) "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)")

In the post-war years, the DR in East Germany continued to develop independently of the DB, but very much in parallel. The [locomotive classification scheme](/wiki/DR_locomotive_classification "DR locomotive classification"), based on that of the [DRG](/wiki/DRG_renumbering_plan_for_steam_locomotives "DRG renumbering plan for steam locomotives"), was extended. The production, conversion and development of [steam locomotives](/wiki/Steam_locomotives "Steam locomotives") initially continued in earnest; older, especially ex-*[Länderbahn](/wiki/L%C3%A4nderbahn "Länderbahn")* classes being rationalised and withdrawn from service. A major conversion (*[Rekonstruktion](/wiki/Rekolokomotive "Rekolokomotive")*) programme to update steam locomotives and rectify flawed, mainly [wartime austerity](/wiki/Kriegslokomotive "Kriegslokomotive"), classes was carried out in the 1950s. Gradually, however, they were replaced by the more economical and easier-to-maintain [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") and [electric](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive") classes. In general this happened rather later than in the West. In 1970, the DR [renumbered](/wiki/DR_locomotive_classification "DR locomotive classification") its locomotives in order to conform to new computerised data standards.

On 3 October 1990, the GDR states acceded to the Federal Republic of Germany. Initially the two railway administrations continued to operate separately, albeit with increasing cooperation, and in 1994 they were merged to form the new [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn").
